metadata.content: As a first-generation student and the only one of my family to make it to college, I wish I knew how heavy the pressure would feel. My parents came to this country at my age without knowing the language, and I carry that with me every day. It makes me feel like I can't fail because they sacrificed so much for me to have opportunities they didn't. The pressure can be very hard sometimes, but I've learned to use it to push myself and thrive instead of letting it break me. I remind myself that their journey and mine are different, and I don't have to be perfect. I would tell other first-gen students that it's okay to feel the pressure, but don't let it consume you and use it as a way to motivate you.
metadata.summary: A first-generation college student grapples with the immense pressure stemming from their immigrant parents' sacrifices, feeling an overwhelming need not to fail. They've learned to transform this weight into motivation, using it to push themselves to thrive rather than be consumed by it. The author advises fellow first-gen students to embrace the pressure as a catalyst for growth, emphasizing that perfection isn't necessary.

metadata.content: One thing I really wish I knew before college was how expensive the small things would be. I knew tuition was a big deal, but I didn't think enough about textbooks, food, late night takeout, school supplies, club fees and random costs that keep showing up. As a first gen student, I also felt guilty about spending money because I knew how much my family was already sacrificing for me to even be here. I was embarrassed to talk about it, so I just tried to handle it all quietly. What I wish I knew is that budgeting really makes a huge difference. Even if it is just tracking where your money goes every week. I also wish I had known sooner that there are campus resources, cheaper textbook options and people who can actually help.
metadata.summary: A first-gen college student shares insights on the unexpected financial challenges of 'small' college expenses beyond tuition, such as textbooks and club fees. They describe feeling guilty and handling financial stress silently due to family sacrifices. The author emphasizes the importance of budgeting and utilizing campus resources for financial support.

metadata.content: One of the biggest things I wish I knew earlier is how fast college goes by. I spent so much time worrying about doing everything right that I did not always let myself try things that I was interested in. I overthought opportunities, hesitated to apply for things, and sometimes avoided stuff because I was scared of looking inexperienced. Looking back, I wish I had taken more chances and trusted that I did not need to have everything figured out at first. I also wish I had asked more questions and spent less time comparing myself to other people. By the time you get close to graduation, you really realize no one had it all perfectly planned out anyways. And I think my biggest regret is wasting energy trying to seem prepared instead of just learning as I went.
metadata.summary: A first-gen graduate reflects on her college experience, regretting the time spent overthinking opportunities, comparing herself to peers, and trying to appear perfectly prepared. She wishes she had taken more chances, asked questions, and trusted the learning process instead of fearing inexperience. Her biggest lesson is that nobody has it all figured out, and it's better to embrace learning as you go.

metadata.content: For a long time, I thought asking for help in college meant I was falling behind. I assumed office hours were only for students who were either failing really badly or already super confident. As a first generation student, there were a lot of things that nobody had ever explained to me, so I just stayed quiet and tried to figure everything out on my own. That usually made things harder than what they needed to be. And what I wish I knew was that asking for help early is actually one of the smartest things you can do. Professors, advisors, and even classmates can help you way more than you think, but only if you let them. I wasted a lot of time being silent because I thought I had to prove that I could handle everything alone.
metadata.summary: A first-generation college student shares their initial struggle with asking for help, mistakenly believing it was a sign of weakness. They recount trying to navigate college alone, which only made things harder. The narrator now realizes that utilizing professors, advisors, and classmates for support early on is a smart strategy and regrets the time wasted trying to prove self-sufficiency.

metadata.content: I remember sitting in my dorm freshman year scrolling through LinkedIn and it felt like everyone already knew what they were doing. Uh people were talking about internships, networking, referrals and I didn't even know what those words really meant. My parents didn't go to college so I didn't have anyone to ask. I thought I was just behind like I had already somehow failed. Sophomore year I almost didn't apply to any internships because I assumed I wasn't qualified, but one night I randomly went to a career workshop. Mostly for the free food but that's where I learned that no one actually expects to know everything. You're just kind of supposed to figure out as you go. I applied to 27 internships and got rejected from 26, but that one yes changed everything. Oh what I wish I knew earlier is this um everyone looks like they have a plan but most people are just guessing confidently. Uh don't count yourself out before you even try.
metadata.summary: A first-generation college student felt lost freshman year, lacking understanding of career concepts like internships due to a lack of family guidance. Initially battling imposter syndrome, a career workshop revealed that most peers are just confidently figuring things out. Empowered by this insight, they diligently applied for many internships, securing one opportunity that transformed their college journey.
